3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Bar/Line Plots
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Explore how to organize and display data using bar plots and line plots. Learn to interpret visual representations of information.
objectives: Create bar plots to display categorical data
Create line plots to display measurement data
Read and interpret information from plots
Compare quantities shown in plots
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a bar plot of favorite ice cream flavors. Ask: 'How can we tell which flavor is most popular?' Discuss how the height of bars helps us compare.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that bar plots use rectangular bars to show data for categories. For example, if 6 students choose apples and 4 choose bananas, the apple bar is 2 units taller. Show that line plots use X marks to display measurements. For example, if pencil lengths are 5, 5, 6, and 7 inches, place two X marks above 5, one above 6, and one above 7.
outline.guided_practice: In pairs, students create a bar plot showing classroom pet preferences. Then they make a line plot of coin flip results from 10 trials. Check for understanding by asking questions about the plots.
outline.independent_practice: Students survey classmates about favorite pizza toppings and create their own bar plot. They then measure pencil lengths and make a line plot of the data.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'How many more people chose pepperoni over cheese?' 'Which measurement appears most often?' Have students explain their reasoning.
outline.exit_ticket: Students draw a simple bar plot or line plot and answer one comparison question about their data.

3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Interpreting Tables/Charts
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Learn to read and understand data presented in tables and charts. Practice finding information and making comparisons.
objectives: Locate specific data in tables and charts
Compare quantities in different rows or columns
Answer questions using table data
Identify patterns in tabular data
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a simple table of classroom supplies. Ask: 'How many pencils do we have?' 'Do we have more pens or erasers?' Let students practice finding information.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain table structure: rows are horizontal, columns are vertical. Show how to find data by matching row and column. For example, if the row for Mia and the column for apples meet at 3, Mia chose 3 apples. In a chart, compare values by reading labels and amounts carefully.
outline.guided_practice: Students work with a class weather table. Together, find the warmest day, coldest day, and which days were similar. Do addition and subtraction with table data.
outline.independent_practice: Students receive a table of sports participation. They answer guided questions: 'Who plays soccer?' 'How many more basketball players than tennis?' 'What's the total?'
outline.check_for_understanding: Have students verbally explain how to find specific pieces of information in a table. Ask them to create their own simple table.
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer 2-3 questions about a provided table without teacher help.

3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Intro Probability (experimental)
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Conduct experiments to explore probability. Record and compare outcomes from flipping coins, rolling dice, and using spinners.
objectives: Conduct simple probability experiments
Record experimental outcomes accurately
Compare frequencies of events
Predict outcomes based on experimental data
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Flip a coin together 10 times. Record heads and tails on the board. Ask: 'Will we get more heads or tails?' Why do we get different amounts each time?
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that probability is about how likely something is to happen. For example, a coin has two possible outcomes, but 10 flips might still give 6 heads and 4 tails. Show dice rolls or spinner spins and record every outcome so students can compare what happened to what they expected.
outline.guided_practice: In small groups, students flip coins 20 times and record results. Roll dice and make tally marks. Compare group results. Discuss why results differ.
outline.independent_practice: Students choose an experiment (coin, dice, or spinner) and conduct 15-20 trials. They create a chart or table showing their results.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What happened most often?' 'If you did this again, would you expect the same result?' Have them explain their thinking.
outline.exit_ticket: Students draw a simple tally chart from their experiment and write one observation about what happened.

3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Mean/Median/Mode
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Find the mean, median, and mode of a data set. Compare how each measure describes the center or most common value.
objectives: Find the mean by adding and dividing
Find the median by ordering data
Identify the mode as the most common value
Compare which measure best describes a set of data
3-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Write 3, 5, 5, 8, 9 on the board. Ask: 'Which number shows up most?' 'Which number is in the middle?' 'What happens if we share the total equally?'
outline.direct_instruction: Mean is the equal share: add the numbers and divide by how many numbers there are. Median is the middle number after putting the data in order. Mode is the value that appears most often. For example, in 2, 4, 4, 6, 9, the mode is 4, the median is 4, and the mean is 25 divided by 5, which equals 5.
outline.guided_practice: Order small data sets together and find mean, median, and mode step by step. For example, use 6, 7, 7, 10: the mode is 7, the median is 7, and the mean is 30 divided by 4, which equals 7.5.
outline.independent_practice: Students solve 3-4 short data sets by finding mean, median, and mode. They explain which measure best describes a set such as daily step counts or quiz scores.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'Why do we put the numbers in order before finding the median?' 'Can a data set have more than one mode?' 'Which measure uses every number in the set?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students find at least two of the three measures for one short data set and explain how they found them.

3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.title: Angles & Lines
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.summary: Identify and measure angles. Classify lines as parallel, intersecting, or perpendicular. Explore angles in real objects.
objectives: Identify different types of angles
Measure angles using a protractor
Recognize parallel and perpendicular lines
Find angles in geometric figures
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a clock face. Ask: 'What angle do the hands make at 3 o'clock?' Point to a right angle in the classroom. Have students find right angles around them.
outline.direct_instruction: An angle is formed by two rays sharing a point. Right angles are 90°. Acute angles are smaller than 90°. Obtuse angles are larger. Show parallel lines (never meet) and perpendicular lines (meet at right angles).
outline.guided_practice: Students use a protractor to measure angles you've drawn. Identify angles in classroom objects. Draw parallel and perpendicular lines on the board together.
outline.independent_practice: Students measure 5 angles on a handout. Classify each as acute, right, or obtuse. Draw one example of parallel lines and one of perpendicular lines.
outline.check_for_understanding: Show an angle and ask students to estimate the measure before measuring. Discuss if their estimate was close.
outline.exit_ticket: Students identify whether given lines are parallel or perpendicular, and classify one angle they see in the classroom.
materials: Images of angles in real objects

3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.title: Area/Perimeter
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.summary: Understand the difference between area and perimeter. Measure and calculate these for rectangles, squares, and other shapes.
objectives: Calculate perimeter by adding all sides
Calculate area by multiplying length and width
Solve real-world area and perimeter problems
Apply formulas to different shapes
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Walk around the perimeter of the classroom. Count your steps. Then ask: 'How much space is inside?' Explain: perimeter is the distance around, area is the space inside.
outline.direct_instruction: Perimeter = distance around (add all sides). Area = space inside (multiply length × width for rectangles). Show examples with grid paper: count squares for area.
outline.guided_practice: Find the perimeter of a rectangle by measuring and adding. Find the area by multiplying dimensions. Do several examples together on the board.
outline.independent_practice: Students measure a rectangle and calculate both perimeter and area. Try different rectangles to see how changes affect each measurement.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'Does a bigger perimeter always mean a bigger area?' (No!) Have them test with different rectangles.
outline.exit_ticket: Given a rectangle's dimensions, students calculate perimeter and area. Write or draw which one is larger.

3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-c.title: Coordinate Plane (Quadrant I)
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-c.summary: Plot points on a coordinate plane using ordered pairs. Move and identify locations using coordinates.
objectives: Understand ordered pairs (x, y)
Plot points on a coordinate plane
Read coordinates of plotted points
Move between locations using coordinates
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-c.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a coordinate plane with one point plotted. Ask: 'Where is this point?' Explain the x-axis (left-right) and y-axis (up-down).
outline.direct_instruction: An ordered pair (x, y) tells location. Start at the origin (0, 0), move right along the x-axis, then move up along the y-axis. In Quadrant I, both numbers are positive. For example, (2, 3) means move 2 right and 3 up.
outline.guided_practice: Plot points together: (2, 3), (4, 1), (1, 4). Name the coordinates of points already plotted. Play a game finding treasure at coordinates.
outline.independent_practice: Students plot 5-8 given points on graph paper. Then name the coordinates of points you show them. Create their own shape using coordinates.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What are the coordinates of that point?' 'Is (3, 2) the same as (2, 3)?' Have them explain why order matters.
outline.exit_ticket: Plot 2-3 points on a coordinate plane and identify the location of 1-2 given points.

3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.title: Transformations (intro)
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.summary: Explore rotations, reflections, and translations. Discover how shapes move and change in the plane.
objectives: Identify rotations, reflections, and translations
Perform transformations on shapes
Understand congruence through transformations
Describe transformations using direction and amount
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a shape. Flip it (reflection). Rotate it (turn). Slide it (translation). Ask: 'What changed? What stayed the same?'
outline.direct_instruction: Translation means slide a shape without turning or flipping it. Rotation means turn a shape around a point. Reflection means flip a shape over a line. For example, sliding a square 3 units right changes its position but not its size or shape.
outline.guided_practice: Using cut-out shapes and mirrors, demonstrate each transformation. Have students copy and predict what happens.
outline.independent_practice: Students perform transformations on grid paper. Draw a triangle, then show it translated right, rotated 90°, and reflected.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I translate a square, is it still a square?' 'How is a reflection different from a rotation?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students identify the transformation shown and describe it (e.g., 'Slide 3 units right').

3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.title: Volume
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.summary: Measure how much space objects take up. Calculate volume of rectangular prisms using unit cubes and formulas.
objectives: Understand volume as cubic units
Count unit cubes to find volume
Use the formula length × width × height
Solve volume problems with real objects
3-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Fill a small box with unit cubes. Count them together. Explain: this number is the volume—how much space is inside.
outline.direct_instruction: Volume is measured in cubic units (cm³, in³). Fill a rectangular prism with cubes and count, or multiply length × width × height. For example, a prism that is 2 cubes long, 3 cubes wide, and 4 cubes tall has 2 × 3 × 4 = 24 cubic units.
outline.guided_practice: Build rectangular prisms with cubes and count the volume together. Try different dimensions. See how volume changes.
outline.independent_practice: Students build 3-4 different rectangular prisms and calculate volume by counting cubes and by formula.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I double the height, what happens to volume?' Have them test.
outline.exit_ticket: Given dimensions, students calculate volume or count cubes for a provided figure.

3-mathematics-number-and-operations-deci.title: Decimals (intro)
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-deci.summary: Connect fractions and decimals. Understand place value to the tenths and hundredths. Read, write, and compare decimal numbers.
objectives: Understand decimals as fractions with denominators of 10 or 100
Read and write decimals
Compare and order decimals
Use decimals in real contexts (money, measurement)
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-deci.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a dollar bill and a dime. Explain: a dime is 0.1 dollars, or 1/10. Connect to decimal notation.
outline.direct_instruction: The decimal point separates whole numbers from parts. Tenths place is one position after the point. Hundredths is two positions after the point. For example, 0.3 means 3 tenths and 0.25 means 25 hundredths. 0.5 = 5/10 = 1/2.
outline.guided_practice: Shade decimal grids to represent 0.3, 0.25, etc. Write decimals and fractions that match. Compare decimals: is 0.5 bigger than 0.3?
outline.independent_practice: Students write decimals for shaded grids. Compare decimal numbers. Use decimals to record measurements and money amounts.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What is 0.7 as a fraction?' 'Is 0.25 the same as 1/4?' Have them explain.
outline.exit_ticket: Students write a decimal and its matching fraction, or identify the larger of two decimals.

3-mathematics-number-and-operations-frac.title: Fractions (concepts, equivalence)
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-frac.summary: Explore parts of a whole. Understand unit fractions, equal shares, and equivalent fractions.
objectives: Understand fractions as equal parts
Read and write fractions
Identify equivalent fractions
Compare fractions with same denominators
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-frac.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Divide a pizza into equal slices. Ask: 'How many slices is the whole pizza?' 'If I eat 2 slices out of 8, what fraction did I eat?'
outline.direct_instruction: A fraction names equal parts of a whole. Numerator (top) tells how many parts. Denominator (bottom) tells how many equal parts total. For example, 3/4 means 3 of 4 equal parts.
outline.guided_practice: Shade fraction models to represent 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, etc. Show equivalent fractions (1/2 = 2/4). Compare 1/2 and 1/3.
outline.independent_practice: Students shade models for given fractions. Find equivalent fractions. Compare fractions with same denominators.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'Is 1/2 bigger than 1/3?' 'Are 2/4 and 1/2 the same?' Have them explain their reasoning.
outline.exit_ticket: Students shade a model to show a fraction and write it, or identify equivalent fractions.

3-mathematics-number-and-operations-mult.title: Multiplication/Division
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-mult.summary: Understand multiplication and division as equal groups, arrays, and related operations. Solve problems by connecting multiplication facts and division facts.
objectives: Represent multiplication and division with equal groups or arrays
Use related multiplication and division facts
Solve multiplication and division word problems
Explain answers with drawings or equations
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-mult.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show 3 bags with 4 apples each. Ask: 'How many total?' Introduce: 3 × 4 = 12.
outline.direct_instruction: Multiplication is repeated addition. For example, 3 × 4 means 4 + 4 + 4 = 12. Division splits a total into equal groups. For example, 12 ÷ 3 = 4 means 12 objects shared into 3 equal groups gives 4 in each group. Arrays can model both operations.
outline.guided_practice: Create arrays with objects or drawings. Write multiplication and matching division sentences. For example, a 3-by-4 array shows both 3 × 4 = 12 and 12 ÷ 3 = 4.
outline.independent_practice: Students create arrays or equal-group drawings for given facts. They solve word problems involving multiplication and division and write an equation to match each story.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'How does 3 × 4 = 12 help you solve 12 ÷ 3?' 'Is 3 × 4 the same as 4 × 3?' Have them draw arrays to explain.
outline.exit_ticket: Students draw an array or equal groups to match one multiplication or division sentence.

3-mathematics-number-and-operations-plac.title: Place Value (thousands/millions)
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-plac.summary: Understand the value of digits in numbers. Work with thousands, millions, and other place values.
objectives: Identify place value of digits (ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, etc.)
Read and write numbers in expanded form
Compare numbers using place value
Round numbers to different places
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-plac.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Write 345. Ask: 'What does the 4 represent?' (40) 'The 3?' (300) Discuss how the same digit means different amounts.
outline.direct_instruction: Each place is 10 times the place to its right. Ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, and beyond. Show with place value blocks or charts. For example, in 4,582, the 4 means 4 thousands, the 5 means 5 hundreds, and the 8 means 8 tens.
outline.guided_practice: Write numbers in expanded form: 234 = 200 + 30 + 4. Compare numbers using place value. Round to nearest 10 or 100.
outline.independent_practice: Students identify place values in numbers. Write numbers in expanded form. Compare numbers and round.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What is 5 in 5,432?' (5 thousands, or 5,000) 'Is 234 or 324 bigger?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students write a number in expanded form or identify the place value of one digit.

3-mathematics-number-and-operations-roun.title: Rounding & Estimation
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-roun.summary: Round numbers to the nearest 10, 100, or 1000. Estimate sums and differences using rounding.
objectives: Round numbers to different place values
Understand when and why to round
Estimate using rounded numbers
Check reasonableness of answers
3-mathematics-number-and-operations-roun.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Ask: 'Is 48 closer to 40 or 50?' Show a number line. Round 48 to 50. Explain using a number line.
outline.direct_instruction: Look at the digit to the right of the place you are rounding to. If it is 5 or more, round up. If it is less than 5, round down. For example, 47 rounds to 50 because it is closer to 50 than 40.
outline.guided_practice: Round several numbers together: 23 to nearest 10, 156 to nearest 100. Use number lines.
outline.independent_practice: Students round 8-10 numbers to specified place values. Estimate sums by rounding first.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What is 67 rounded to the nearest 10?' 'Is that reasonable?' Have them explain their thinking.
outline.exit_ticket: Students round 2 numbers and optionally estimate a sum.

outline.independent_practice: Students perform transformations on grid paper. Draw a triangle, then show it translated right, rotated 90°, and reflected.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I translate a square, is it still a square?' 'How is a reflection different from a rotation?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students identify the transformation shown and describe it (e.g., 'Slide 3 units right').

4-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.title: Volume
4-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.summary: Measure how much space objects take up. Calculate volume of rectangular prisms using unit cubes and formulas.
objectives: Understand volume as cubic units
Count unit cubes to find volume
Use the formula length × width × height
Solve volume problems with real objects
4-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Fill a small box with unit cubes. Count them together. Explain: this number is the volume—how much space is inside.
outline.direct_instruction: Volume is measured in cubic units (cm³, in³). Fill a rectangular prism with cubes and count, or multiply length × width × height. For example, a prism that is 2 cubes long, 3 cubes wide, and 4 cubes tall has 2 × 3 × 4 = 24 cubic units.
outline.guided_practice: Build rectangular prisms with cubes and count the volume together. Try different dimensions. See how volume changes.
outline.independent_practice: Students build 3-4 different rectangular prisms and calculate volume by counting cubes and by formula.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I double the height, what happens to volume?' Have them test.
outline.exit_ticket: Given dimensions, students calculate volume or count cubes for a provided figure.

4-mathematics-number-and-operations-deci.title: Decimals (intro)
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-deci.summary: Connect fractions and decimals. Understand place value to the tenths and hundredths. Read, write, and compare decimal numbers.
objectives: Understand decimals as fractions with denominators of 10 or 100
Read and write decimals
Compare and order decimals
Use decimals in real contexts (money, measurement)
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-deci.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a dollar bill and a dime. Explain: a dime is 0.1 dollars, or 1/10. Connect to decimal notation.
outline.direct_instruction: The decimal point separates whole numbers from parts. Tenths place is one position after the point. Hundredths is two positions after the point. For example, 0.3 means 3 tenths and 0.25 means 25 hundredths. 0.5 = 5/10 = 1/2.
outline.guided_practice: Shade decimal grids to represent 0.3, 0.25, etc. Write decimals and fractions that match. Compare decimals: is 0.5 bigger than 0.3?
outline.independent_practice: Students write decimals for shaded grids. Compare decimal numbers. Use decimals to record measurements and money amounts.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What is 0.7 as a fraction?' 'Is 0.25 the same as 1/4?' Have them explain.
outline.exit_ticket: Students write a decimal and its matching fraction, or identify the larger of two decimals.

4-mathematics-number-and-operations-frac.title: Fractions (concepts, equivalence)
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-frac.summary: Explore parts of a whole. Understand unit fractions, equal shares, and equivalent fractions.
objectives: Understand fractions as equal parts
Read and write fractions
Identify equivalent fractions
Compare fractions with same denominators
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-frac.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Divide a pizza into equal slices. Ask: 'How many slices is the whole pizza?' 'If I eat 2 slices out of 8, what fraction did I eat?'
outline.direct_instruction: A fraction names equal parts of a whole. Numerator (top) tells how many parts. Denominator (bottom) tells how many equal parts total. For example, 3/4 means 3 of 4 equal parts.
outline.guided_practice: Shade fraction models to represent 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, etc. Show equivalent fractions (1/2 = 2/4). Compare 1/2 and 1/3.
outline.independent_practice: Students shade models for given fractions. Find equivalent fractions. Compare fractions with same denominators.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'Is 1/2 bigger than 1/3?' 'Are 2/4 and 1/2 the same?' Have them explain their reasoning.
outline.exit_ticket: Students shade a model to show a fraction and write it, or identify equivalent fractions.

4-mathematics-number-and-operations-mult.title: Multiplication/Division
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-mult.summary: Understand multiplication and division as equal groups, arrays, and related operations. Solve problems by connecting multiplication facts and division facts.
objectives: Represent multiplication and division with equal groups or arrays
Use related multiplication and division facts
Solve multiplication and division word problems
Explain answers with drawings or equations
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-mult.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show 3 bags with 4 apples each. Ask: 'How many total?' Introduce: 3 × 4 = 12.
outline.direct_instruction: Multiplication is repeated addition. For example, 3 × 4 means 4 + 4 + 4 = 12. Division splits a total into equal groups. For example, 12 ÷ 3 = 4 means 12 objects shared into 3 equal groups gives 4 in each group. Arrays can model both operations.
outline.guided_practice: Create arrays with objects or drawings. Write multiplication and matching division sentences. For example, a 3-by-4 array shows both 3 × 4 = 12 and 12 ÷ 3 = 4.
outline.independent_practice: Students create arrays or equal-group drawings for given facts. They solve word problems involving multiplication and division and write an equation to match each story.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'How does 3 × 4 = 12 help you solve 12 ÷ 3?' 'Is 3 × 4 the same as 4 × 3?' Have them draw arrays to explain.
outline.exit_ticket: Students draw an array or equal groups to match one multiplication or division sentence.

4-mathematics-number-and-operations-plac.title: Place Value (thousands/millions)
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-plac.summary: Understand the value of digits in numbers. Work with thousands, millions, and other place values.
objectives: Identify place value of digits (ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, etc.)
Read and write numbers in expanded form
Compare numbers using place value
Round numbers to different places
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-plac.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Write 345. Ask: 'What does the 4 represent?' (40) 'The 3?' (300) Discuss how the same digit means different amounts.
outline.direct_instruction: Each place is 10 times the place to its right. Ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, and beyond. Show with place value blocks or charts. For example, in 4,582, the 4 means 4 thousands, the 5 means 5 hundreds, and the 8 means 8 tens.
outline.guided_practice: Write numbers in expanded form: 234 = 200 + 30 + 4. Compare numbers using place value. Round to nearest 10 or 100.
outline.independent_practice: Students identify place values in numbers. Write numbers in expanded form. Compare numbers and round.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What is 5 in 5,432?' (5 thousands, or 5,000) 'Is 234 or 324 bigger?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students write a number in expanded form or identify the place value of one digit.

4-mathematics-number-and-operations-roun.title: Rounding & Estimation
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-roun.summary: Round numbers to the nearest 10, 100, or 1000. Estimate sums and differences using rounding.
objectives: Round numbers to different place values
Understand when and why to round
Estimate using rounded numbers
Check reasonableness of answers
4-mathematics-number-and-operations-roun.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Ask: 'Is 48 closer to 40 or 50?' Show a number line. Round 48 to 50. Explain using a number line.
outline.direct_instruction: Look at the digit to the right of the place you are rounding to. If it is 5 or more, round up. If it is less than 5, round down. For example, 47 rounds to 50 because it is closer to 50 than 40.
outline.guided_practice: Round several numbers together: 23 to nearest 10, 156 to nearest 100. Use number lines.
outline.independent_practice: Students round 8-10 numbers to specified place values. Estimate sums by rounding first.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What is 67 rounded to the nearest 10?' 'Is that reasonable?' Have them explain their thinking.
outline.exit_ticket: Students round 2 numbers and optionally estimate a sum.

5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Bar/Line Plots
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Explore how to organize and display data using bar plots and line plots. Learn to interpret visual representations of information.
objectives: Create bar plots to display categorical data
Create line plots to display measurement data
Read and interpret information from plots
Compare quantities shown in plots
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a bar plot of favorite ice cream flavors. Ask: 'How can we tell which flavor is most popular?' Discuss how the height of bars helps us compare.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that bar plots use rectangular bars to show data for categories. For example, if 6 students choose apples and 4 choose bananas, the apple bar is 2 units taller. Show that line plots use X marks to display measurements. For example, if pencil lengths are 5, 5, 6, and 7 inches, place two X marks above 5, one above 6, and one above 7.
outline.guided_practice: In pairs, students create a bar plot showing classroom pet preferences. Then they make a line plot of coin flip results from 10 trials. Check for understanding by asking questions about the plots.
outline.independent_practice: Students survey classmates about favorite pizza toppings and create their own bar plot. They then measure pencil lengths and make a line plot of the data.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'How many more people chose pepperoni over cheese?' 'Which measurement appears most often?' Have students explain their reasoning.
outline.exit_ticket: Students draw a simple bar plot or line plot and answer one comparison question about their data.

5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Interpreting Tables/Charts
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Learn to read and understand data presented in tables and charts. Practice finding information and making comparisons.
objectives: Locate specific data in tables and charts
Compare quantities in different rows or columns
Answer questions using table data
Identify patterns in tabular data
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a simple table of classroom supplies. Ask: 'How many pencils do we have?' 'Do we have more pens or erasers?' Let students practice finding information.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain table structure: rows are horizontal, columns are vertical. Show how to find data by matching row and column. For example, if the row for Mia and the column for apples meet at 3, Mia chose 3 apples. In a chart, compare values by reading labels and amounts carefully.
outline.guided_practice: Students work with a class weather table. Together, find the warmest day, coldest day, and which days were similar. Do addition and subtraction with table data.
outline.independent_practice: Students receive a table of sports participation. They answer guided questions: 'Who plays soccer?' 'How many more basketball players than tennis?' 'What's the total?'
outline.check_for_understanding: Have students verbally explain how to find specific pieces of information in a table. Ask them to create their own simple table.
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer 2-3 questions about a provided table without teacher help.

5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Intro Probability (experimental)
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Conduct experiments to explore probability. Record and compare outcomes from flipping coins, rolling dice, and using spinners.
objectives: Conduct simple probability experiments
Record experimental outcomes accurately
Compare frequencies of events
Predict outcomes based on experimental data
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Flip a coin together 10 times. Record heads and tails on the board. Ask: 'Will we get more heads or tails?' Why do we get different amounts each time?
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that probability is about how likely something is to happen. For example, a coin has two possible outcomes, but 10 flips might still give 6 heads and 4 tails. Show dice rolls or spinner spins and record every outcome so students can compare what happened to what they expected.
outline.guided_practice: In small groups, students flip coins 20 times and record results. Roll dice and make tally marks. Compare group results. Discuss why results differ.
outline.independent_practice: Students choose an experiment (coin, dice, or spinner) and conduct 15-20 trials. They create a chart or table showing their results.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What happened most often?' 'If you did this again, would you expect the same result?' Have them explain their thinking.
outline.exit_ticket: Students draw a simple tally chart from their experiment and write one observation about what happened.

5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Mean/Median/Mode
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Find the mean, median, and mode of a data set. Compare how each measure describes the center or most common value.
objectives: Find the mean by adding and dividing
Find the median by ordering data
Identify the mode as the most common value
Compare which measure best describes a set of data
5-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Write 3, 5, 5, 8, 9 on the board. Ask: 'Which number shows up most?' 'Which number is in the middle?' 'What happens if we share the total equally?'
outline.direct_instruction: Mean is the equal share: add the numbers and divide by how many numbers there are. Median is the middle number after putting the data in order. Mode is the value that appears most often. For example, in 2, 4, 4, 6, 9, the mode is 4, the median is 4, and the mean is 25 divided by 5, which equals 5.
outline.guided_practice: Order small data sets together and find mean, median, and mode step by step. For example, use 6, 7, 7, 10: the mode is 7, the median is 7, and the mean is 30 divided by 4, which equals 7.5.
outline.independent_practice: Students solve 3-4 short data sets by finding mean, median, and mode. They explain which measure best describes a set such as daily step counts or quiz scores.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'Why do we put the numbers in order before finding the median?' 'Can a data set have more than one mode?' 'Which measure uses every number in the set?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students find at least two of the three measures for one short data set and explain how they found them.

5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.title: Angles & Lines
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.summary: Identify and measure angles. Classify lines as parallel, intersecting, or perpendicular. Explore angles in real objects.
objectives: Identify different types of angles
Measure angles using a protractor
Recognize parallel and perpendicular lines
Find angles in geometric figures
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a clock face. Ask: 'What angle do the hands make at 3 o'clock?' Point to a right angle in the classroom. Have students find right angles around them.
outline.direct_instruction: An angle is formed by two rays sharing a point. Right angles are 90°. Acute angles are smaller than 90°. Obtuse angles are larger. Show parallel lines (never meet) and perpendicular lines (meet at right angles).
outline.guided_practice: Students use a protractor to measure angles you've drawn. Identify angles in classroom objects. Draw parallel and perpendicular lines on the board together.
outline.independent_practice: Students measure 5 angles on a handout. Classify each as acute, right, or obtuse. Draw one example of parallel lines and one of perpendicular lines.
outline.check_for_understanding: Show an angle and ask students to estimate the measure before measuring. Discuss if their estimate was close.
outline.exit_ticket: Students identify whether given lines are parallel or perpendicular, and classify one angle they see in the classroom.
materials: Images of angles in real objects

5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.title: Area/Perimeter
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.summary: Understand the difference between area and perimeter. Measure and calculate these for rectangles, squares, and other shapes.
objectives: Calculate perimeter by adding all sides
Calculate area by multiplying length and width
Solve real-world area and perimeter problems
Apply formulas to different shapes
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-a.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Walk around the perimeter of the classroom. Count your steps. Then ask: 'How much space is inside?' Explain: perimeter is the distance around, area is the space inside.
outline.direct_instruction: Perimeter = distance around (add all sides). Area = space inside (multiply length × width for rectangles). Show examples with grid paper: count squares for area.
outline.guided_practice: Find the perimeter of a rectangle by measuring and adding. Find the area by multiplying dimensions. Do several examples together on the board.
outline.independent_practice: Students measure a rectangle and calculate both perimeter and area. Try different rectangles to see how changes affect each measurement.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'Does a bigger perimeter always mean a bigger area?' (No!) Have them test with different rectangles.
outline.exit_ticket: Given a rectangle's dimensions, students calculate perimeter and area. Write or draw which one is larger.

5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-c.title: Coordinate Plane (Quadrant I)
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-c.summary: Plot points on a coordinate plane using ordered pairs. Move and identify locations using coordinates.
objectives: Understand ordered pairs (x, y)
Plot points on a coordinate plane
Read coordinates of plotted points
Move between locations using coordinates
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-c.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a coordinate plane with one point plotted. Ask: 'Where is this point?' Explain the x-axis (left-right) and y-axis (up-down).
outline.direct_instruction: An ordered pair (x, y) tells location. Start at the origin (0, 0), move right along the x-axis, then move up along the y-axis. In Quadrant I, both numbers are positive. For example, (2, 3) means move 2 right and 3 up.
outline.guided_practice: Plot points together: (2, 3), (4, 1), (1, 4). Name the coordinates of points already plotted. Play a game finding treasure at coordinates.
outline.independent_practice: Students plot 5-8 given points on graph paper. Then name the coordinates of points you show them. Create their own shape using coordinates.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What are the coordinates of that point?' 'Is (3, 2) the same as (2, 3)?' Have them explain why order matters.
outline.exit_ticket: Plot 2-3 points on a coordinate plane and identify the location of 1-2 given points.

5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.title: Transformations (intro)
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.summary: Explore rotations, reflections, and translations. Discover how shapes move and change in the plane.
objectives: Identify rotations, reflections, and translations
Perform transformations on shapes
Understand congruence through transformations
Describe transformations using direction and amount
5-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a shape. Flip it (reflection). Rotate it (turn). Slide it (translation). Ask: 'What changed? What stayed the same?'
outline.direct_instruction: Translation means slide a shape without turning or flipping it. Rotation means turn a shape around a point. Reflection means flip a shape over a line. For example, sliding a square 3 units right changes its position but not its size or shape.
outline.guided_practice: Using cut-out shapes and mirrors, demonstrate each transformation. Have students copy and predict what happens.
outline.independent_practice: Students perform transformations on grid paper. Draw a triangle, then show it translated right, rotated 90°, and reflected.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I translate a square, is it still a square?' 'How is a reflection different from a rotation?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students identify the transformation shown and describe it (e.g., 'Slide 3 units right').

Count unit cubes to find volume
Use the formula length × width × height

outline.exit_ticket: Given dimensions, students calculate volume or count cubes for a provided figure.

6-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Probability Rules

objectives: Conduct simple probability experiments
Record experimental outcomes accurately
Compare frequencies of events
Predict outcomes based on experimental data
6-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Flip a coin together 10 times. Record heads and tails on the board. Ask: 'Will we get more heads or tails?' Why do we get different amounts each time?
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that probability is about likelihood. Even though a coin has two sides, we might not get exactly equal results. Show dice rolls or spinner spins. Record every outcome.
outline.guided_practice: In small groups, students flip coins 20 times and record results. Roll dice and make tally marks. Compare group results. Discuss why results differ.

Record experimental outcomes accurately
Compare frequencies of events

outline.hook: Show a shape. Flip it (reflection). Rotate it (turn). Slide it (translation). Ask: 'What changed? What stayed the same?'
outline.direct_instruction: Translation: slide a shape without rotating or flipping. Rotation: turn a shape around a point. Reflection: flip a shape over a line.

outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I translate a square, is it still a square?' 'How is a reflection different from a rotation?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students identify the transformation shown and describe it (e.g., 'Slide 3 units right').

Count unit cubes to find volume
Use the formula length × width × height

6-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Fill a small box with unit cubes. Count them together. Explain: this number is the volume—how much space is inside.
outline.direct_instruction: Volume is measured in cubic units (cm³, in³). Fill a rectangular prism with cubes and count. Or multiply: length × width × height.
outline.guided_practice: Build rectangular prisms with cubes and count the volume together. Try different dimensions. See how volume changes.
outline.independent_practice: Students build 3-4 different rectangular prisms and calculate volume by counting cubes and by formula.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I double the height, what happens to volume?' Have them test.
outline.exit_ticket: Given dimensions, students calculate volume or count cubes for a provided figure.

6-mathematics-number-and-operations-rati.title: Ratios & Proportional Reasoning
6-mathematics-number-and-operations-rati.summary: Compare quantities with ratios, equivalent ratios, and unit rates in contexts such as recipes, prices, maps, and classroom surveys.
objectives: Interpret what a ratio compares and keep quantities in the correct order
Use tables, scale factors, and double number lines to build equivalent ratios
Find and explain unit rates in real-world situations
Decide whether a ratio relationship is proportional and justify the reasoning
6-mathematics-number-and-operations-rati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show two snack mix recipes: 2 cups pretzels to 3 cups cereal and 4 cups pretzels to 6 cups cereal. Ask whether the recipes would taste the same and what each ratio is comparing.
outline.direct_instruction: Define a ratio as a comparison of two quantities with labels attached. Model equivalent ratios by scaling both parts of 2:3 to make 4:6 and 6:9. For example, if 3 notebooks cost $9, then the unit rate is $3 per notebook, which helps predict that 5 notebooks would cost $15.
outline.guided_practice: Build a ratio table and a double number line for a recipe that uses 2 cups juice for every 5 cups water. Work together to find matching amounts for 4 cups juice, 6 cups juice, and 1 cup juice, and discuss which method makes the structure easiest to see.
outline.independent_practice: Students solve ratio problems about maps, classroom surveys, and prices. Include one problem where they scale a ratio, one where they find a unit rate, and one where they explain whether two tables represent a proportional relationship.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask students to explain why 4:6 and 6:9 are equivalent but 4:7 is not. Have them describe the multiplicative pattern rather than only the additive change.
outline.exit_ticket: A trail map uses 1 inch to represent 4 miles. Students determine how many miles 3 inches represents and write one sentence explaining why the relationship is proportional.
materials: Practice problems with prices and map scales

8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Probability Rules
8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Analyze compound events by organizing sample spaces, counting favorable outcomes, and using complements when that strategy is more efficient.
objectives: Represent compound events with lists, tables, or organized sample spaces
Calculate probabilities of compound events from total and favorable outcomes
Use the complement rule for events such as not, at least one, or everything except
Explain why an answer is reasonable based on the structure of the sample space
8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Ask students to predict the chance of flipping heads and then rolling a number greater than 4. Let them estimate first, then discuss why a compound event needs an organized list of outcomes.
outline.direct_instruction: Model compound probability with a sample space. For example, if a coin is flipped and a number cube is rolled, there are 12 equally likely outcomes. The event heads and a number greater than 4 has 2 favorable outcomes, so the probability is 2/12 or 1/6. Then show how the complement rule can find the probability of not getting a sum of 7 when rolling two number cubes.
outline.guided_practice: Create a table for rolling two number cubes and identify all combinations that make sums of 6, 7, and 8. Compare a direct count to a complement strategy and discuss which method is more efficient for each question.
outline.independent_practice: Students solve compound-event tasks involving coins, number cubes, cards, and spinners. Include one item that uses an organized sample space, one that uses a table, and one that uses the complement of an event such as at least one success.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask why the probability of a sum of 7 on two number cubes is 6/36 instead of 6/12. Listen for students to name the full sample space and not just the number of possible sums.
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: A fair coin is flipped twice. What is the probability of getting at least one head? They must solve it and state whether they used direct counting or a complement.

8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Sampling & Inference (intro)
8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Conduct experiments to explore probability. Record and compare outcomes from flipping coins, rolling dice, and using spinners.
objectives: Conduct simple probability experiments
Record experimental outcomes accurately
Compare frequencies of events
Predict outcomes based on experimental data
8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Flip a coin together 10 times. Record heads and tails on the board. Ask: 'Will we get more heads or tails?' Why do we get different amounts each time?
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that probability is about likelihood. Even though a coin has two sides, we might not get exactly equal results. Show dice rolls or spinner spins. Record every outcome.
outline.guided_practice: In small groups, students flip coins 20 times and record results. Roll dice and make tally marks. Compare group results. Discuss why results differ.
outline.independent_practice: Students choose an experiment (coin, dice, or spinner) and conduct 15-20 trials. They create a chart or table showing their results.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'What happened most often?' 'If you did this again, would you expect the same result?' Have them explain their thinking.
outline.exit_ticket: Students draw a simple tally chart from their experiment and write one observation about what happened.

8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.title: Scatterplots & Correlation
8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.summary: Interpret scatterplots by describing association, identifying outliers, drawing informal lines of fit, and using trends to make reasonable predictions.
objectives: Describe the direction and strength of association shown in a scatterplot
Identify outliers and explain how they affect the overall interpretation
Draw an informal line of fit that represents the center of a data pattern
Use a line of fit to make predictions and judge whether the prediction is reasonable
8-mathematics-data-and-probability-stati.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Display a scatterplot showing hours studied and quiz scores. Ask what students notice first: whether the points rise or fall, whether they stay close together, and whether any point looks unusual.
outline.direct_instruction: Teach students to read direction, strength, and outliers before making predictions. For example, if points like (1, 58), (2, 64), (3, 70), and (4, 76) rise from left to right, the scatterplot shows a positive association. Model how an informal line of fit should pass through the center of the data with a similar amount of scatter above and below the line.
outline.guided_practice: Work through a scatterplot of practice time and performance score. Students describe the correlation, sketch a reasonable line of fit, and predict the score for a nearby x-value such as 6 hours of practice. Discuss why predictions inside the data range are usually stronger than predictions far outside it.
outline.independent_practice: Students analyze several scatterplots about science data, sports data, and classroom surveys. For each graph, they identify the association, note any outlier, and use a line of fit to make one reasonable prediction.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask students to compare two scatterplots and explain which one shows the stronger positive association. Require them to reference how tightly the points cluster around an imagined line.
outline.exit_ticket: Students examine a small scatterplot and answer two questions: What type of association is shown, and what prediction would you make for a new x-value near the existing data?

8-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.title: Transformations & Symmetry
8-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.summary: Explore rotations, reflections, and translations. Discover how shapes move and change in the plane.
objectives: Identify rotations, reflections, and translations
Perform transformations on shapes
Understand congruence through transformations
Describe transformations using direction and amount
8-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-t.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Show a shape. Flip it (reflection). Rotate it (turn). Slide it (translation). Ask: 'What changed? What stayed the same?'
outline.direct_instruction: Translation: slide a shape without rotating or flipping. Rotation: turn a shape around a point. Reflection: flip a shape over a line.
outline.guided_practice: Using cut-out shapes and mirrors, demonstrate each transformation. Have students copy and predict what happens.
outline.independent_practice: Students perform transformations on grid paper. Draw a triangle, then show it translated right, rotated 90°, and reflected.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I translate a square, is it still a square?' 'How is a reflection different from a rotation?'
outline.exit_ticket: Students identify the transformation shown and describe it (e.g., 'Slide 3 units right').

8-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.title: Volume & Surface Area
8-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.summary: Measure how much space objects take up. Calculate volume of rectangular prisms using unit cubes and formulas.
objectives: Understand volume as cubic units
Count unit cubes to find volume
Use the formula length × width × height
Solve volume problems with real objects
8-mathematics-geometry-and-measurement-v.subject: Mathematics

outline.hook: Fill a small box with unit cubes. Count them together. Explain: this number is the volume—how much space is inside.
outline.direct_instruction: Volume is measured in cubic units (cm³, in³). Fill a rectangular prism with cubes and count. Or multiply: length × width × height.
outline.guided_practice: Build rectangular prisms with cubes and count the volume together. Try different dimensions. See how volume changes.
outline.independent_practice: Students build 3-4 different rectangular prisms and calculate volume by counting cubes and by formula.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask: 'If I double the height, what happens to volume?' Have them test.
outline.exit_ticket: Given dimensions, students calculate volume or count cubes for a provided figure.

4-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.title: Citizenship & Rights Launch Lesson
4-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.summary: Analyze a real First Amendment excerpt to identify protected rights, separate observation from inference, and connect a civic action to evidence from the source.
objectives: Identify who created a civic source, when it was written, and why it matters
Use details from the First Amendment to explain what rights it protects
Separate direct observations from reasonable inferences
Support a civic claim with evidence from the source
4-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask students whether a school should be allowed to stop people from sharing opinions, gathering peacefully, or asking leaders for change. Introduce the First Amendment as a real source that helps answer that question.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to source the First Amendment, identify what it directly says about speech, assembly, and petition, and distinguish observation from inference when explaining why the amendment was written.
outline.guided_practice: Read the source excerpt together. Highlight the protected rights, discuss what those words mean in student-friendly language, and connect a modern civic action to the exact phrase in the source.
outline.independent_practice: Students answer source-analysis questions and use a claim-evidence-reasoning frame to explain which First Amendment right fits a given civic scenario.
outline.check_for_understanding: Ask students to identify one direct observation from the amendment, one reasonable inference, and one phrase that supports a civic claim.
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete the sentence: 'The First Amendment protects ___ because the source says ___.'
